name: Understanding how laws, standards and frameworks apply to AI
tagline: Which instrument owns which obligation. The EU AI Act's four risk tiers and who carries the high-risk duties, GPAI provider duties on a phased timeline, GDPR's grip on personal data inside AI, and the voluntary-vs-certifiable frameworks (NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001 & 22989, OECD).
overview: The legal-and-frameworks core of the AIGP, and the heaviest single domain (blueprint 19-23 questions of 100). It tests whether you can take a scenario and say exactly which body of rules applies and what it demands. Four things dominate: (1) the EU AI Act's risk-tier architecture - unacceptable/prohibited, high-risk, limited-risk (transparency), minimal - and, given a system, which tier it lands in and therefore which obligations attach; (2) who carries each obligation - provider (developer) vs deployer (user), the distinction the exam returns to again and again; (3) GDPR x AI - that data-protection law still governs personal data the moment it enters a prompt, training set, or log, independent of the AI Act; and (4) the framework landscape - NIST AI RMF and OECD are voluntary, ISO/IEC 42001 is a certifiable management system, and only the EU AI Act is binding law. Currency matters here: the Act bites on a published timeline (into force Aug 2024; prohibitions Feb 2025; GPAI Aug 2025; high-risk Annex III 2 Dec 2027; Annex I / Article 6(1) 2 Aug 2028 — the two high-risk dates were deferred from the original Aug 2026 / Aug 2027 by the Digital Omnibus on AI, agreed May 2026), and 'all obligations apply on day one' is a classic distractor. This domain maps to BoK sub-domains II.A (existing data-privacy laws), II.B (other existing laws - non-discrimination, consumer protection, product liability, IP), II.C (the EU AI Act) and II.D (industry standards - OECD, NIST RMF/Playbook, NIST ARIA, ISO 22989 & 42001).
objectives: Classify an AI system into the correct EU AI Act risk tier (unacceptable / high / limited / minimal) and derive the obligations that attach - the single highest-leverage skill in this domain.

List the high-risk obligations (risk management, data governance, technical documentation, record-keeping/logging, transparency to deployers, human oversight, accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity) and assign each to the provider or the deployer.
Distinguish provider (developer) from deployer (user) - and importer/distributor - and explain how obligations differ by role, including for general-purpose AI (GPAI) models.
Explain how GDPR continues to govern personal data used in AI (DPIAs, lawful basis, data minimisation, third-party processors, cross-border transfers, retention) independently of the EU AI Act.

Place each framework on the binding / voluntary / certifiable spectrum - EU AI Act (binding), NIST AI RMF and OECD principles (voluntary), ISO/IEC 42001 (certifiable management system) with ISO/IEC 22989 (terminology) - and pick the instrument that answers a given governance need.
Recall the EU AI Act implementation timeline and match each obligation to the date it starts to apply.

lessons.title: The EU AI Act risk-tier pyramid
lessons.objective: Classify any AI system into the correct EU AI Act tier - unacceptable, high, limited or minimal - and know that GPAI is a separate track.
lessons.body:
## The pyramid the whole exam is built on

The EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) does not regulate 'AI' as one thing. It sorts AI systems by the **risk they pose to health, safety and fundamental rights**, and the obligations scale with the tier. Learn the pyramid cold, because most Domain II questions reduce to *'which tier is this, and therefore what applies?'*

- **Unacceptable risk - prohibited (Article 5).** A short, closed list of practices banned outright. No compliance path exists; you simply may not do them.
- **High risk (Article 6 + Annexes I and III).** Permitted, but only under the heavy obligation set (risk management, data governance, documentation, logging, human oversight, accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity, conformity assessment). This is where the bulk of the Act's text lives.
- **Limited risk - transparency (Article 50).** Permitted with light **disclosure** duties: tell people they are dealing with, or looking at, AI.
- **Minimal or no risk.** Everything else (spam filters, AI in video games). No obligations under the Act; voluntary codes of conduct are encouraged.

> **Exam tip - the number-one distractor:** treating *high-risk* as *banned*. High-risk systems are **allowed**; they just carry obligations. 'Banned' is only the unacceptable tier. If an option says a high-risk system 'must be prohibited', it is wrong.

## Tier 1 - unacceptable risk (the Article 5 prohibitions)

These are **banned** because the risk is deemed incompatible with EU values. The list (Article 5) includes:

- **Harmful manipulation** - subliminal, purposefully manipulative or deceptive techniques that materially distort behaviour and cause significant harm.
- **Exploiting vulnerabilities** - of age, disability, or a specific social or economic situation, to distort behaviour harmfully.
- **Social scoring** - evaluating or classifying people over time based on social behaviour or personal characteristics, leading to unjustified detrimental treatment.
- **Predictive policing based solely on profiling** a person's personality traits.
- **Untargeted scraping** of facial images from the internet or CCTV to build facial-recognition databases.
- **Emotion recognition in the workplace and education** (narrow medical/safety exceptions).
- **Biometric categorisation** to infer sensitive attributes (race, political opinions, trade-union membership, religious/philosophical beliefs, sex life, sexual orientation).
- **Real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces for law enforcement**, save for narrowly defined, authorised exceptions (e.g. a targeted search for a missing person or preventing an imminent terror threat).

> **Exam tip:** you don't need to recite all eight verbatim, but you must **recognise a prohibited practice when a scenario describes one** - and know the ban is absolute (no risk-management workaround makes social scoring legal).

## Tier 2 - high risk: two doors in (Article 6)

An AI system is **high-risk** by one of two routes:

1. **Article 6(1) - safety-component / product route (Annex I).** The AI is a safety component of, or is itself, a product covered by listed EU harmonisation legislation (machinery, medical devices, toys, lifts, etc.) **and** that product must already undergo **third-party conformity assessment**. Think: the AI that controls a surgical robot.
2. **Article 6(2) - use-case route (Annex III).** The AI falls into one of the listed high-stakes **use cases**, regardless of what product it sits in. Annex III areas: **biometrics; critical infrastructure; education and vocational training; employment and worker management; access to essential private and public services** (e.g. credit scoring, benefits, emergency dispatch); **law enforcement; migration, asylum and border control; administration of justice and democratic processes.**

There is a narrow **Article 6(3) filter**: an Annex III system may *not* be high-risk if it performs only a narrow procedural task, improves the result of prior human work, detects decision-patterns without replacing human judgement, or does preparatory work - **but** any system that does **profiling of natural persons is always high-risk**.

> **Exam tip:** Annex III is the door the exam walks through most. If a stem mentions CV-screening, credit decisions, exam grading, or benefits eligibility, your antenna should say **high-risk (Annex III)** immediately - then the question is which *obligation* applies and to *whom*.

## Tier 3 - limited risk (Article 50 transparency)

Not dangerous enough for the high-risk regime, but people deserve to **know AI is involved**. Article 50 duties (covered fully in Lesson 2) include telling users they are interacting with a chatbot, and labelling AI-generated 'deepfake' or synthetic content. Forgetting this tier exists - or assuming a chatbot must be high-risk - is a common error.

## Tier 4 - minimal or no risk

The large majority of AI. No obligations under the Act. The Act *encourages* voluntary codes of conduct but imposes nothing.

## Where GPAI sits

**General-purpose AI (GPAI) models** (think foundation models) are governed by a **separate, parallel track** (Articles 51-55), not by the four-tier pyramid. A GPAI model provider has its own duties, and a subset of very capable models are designated **GPAI with systemic risk** with extra obligations. Keep this mentally distinct: a scenario can involve *both* a GPAI model provider *and* a downstream deployer who has built a high-risk system on top - different rules for each. Lesson 3 handles GPAI in full.

## The one procedure to run on any scenario

1. Is it a **prohibited** practice (Article 5)? -> banned, stop.
2. Is it **Annex I safety-component** or **Annex III use-case** (and not filtered out by 6(3))? -> **high-risk**, full obligations.
3. Does it **interact with people or generate synthetic content** (chatbot, deepfake, emotion recognition)? -> **limited-risk transparency** duties.
4. Otherwise -> **minimal**, no obligations.
5. Separately: is a **GPAI model** in play? -> apply Articles 53/55 to its provider.

Run that ladder and the tier - and therefore the obligations - falls out.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Under the EU AI Act, an AI system used to screen job applicants (an Annex III use case) is classified as:

options.text: High-risk - permitted, but subject to the full obligation set
Unacceptable-risk - prohibited outright
Limited-risk - only a transparency/disclosure duty applies
Minimal-risk - no obligations under the Act

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Employment and worker management is an Annex III high-risk use case, so the system is **high-risk** and carries the full obligations - but it is **permitted**, not banned. It is not unacceptable (that tier is the closed Article 5 list), not merely limited-risk (transparency-only is for chatbots/deepfakes), and certainly not minimal.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Which of the following is a PROHIBITED (unacceptable-risk) practice under Article 5?

options.text: Social scoring of individuals leading to unjustified detrimental treatment
An AI system that scores creditworthiness for a bank
A customer-service chatbot on a retail website
An AI that recommends films to streaming subscribers

knowledgeCheck.explanation: **Social scoring** appears on the Article 5 prohibited list. Credit scoring is **high-risk** (Annex III access-to-essential-services), a chatbot is **limited-risk** (Article 50 transparency), and film recommendation is **minimal-risk**. Only the first is banned outright.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A colleague says: 'Our AI is high-risk, so the EU AI Act means we cannot deploy it at all.' What is the correction?

options.text: High-risk systems are permitted; they must meet the obligation set (risk management, documentation, human oversight, etc.). Only 'unacceptable-risk' practices are banned
Nothing - high-risk does mean the system is prohibited
High-risk only means a transparency label is required, nothing more
High-risk classification only applies to general-purpose AI models

knowledgeCheck.explanation: High-risk is **allowed with obligations**, not banned - conflating high-risk with prohibited is the classic trap. Transparency-only is the *limited-risk* tier, and high-risk applies to Annex I/III systems, not (specifically) to GPAI models, which have their own separate track.

lessons.title: Provider vs deployer, and the high-risk obligation set
lessons.objective: Assign each EU AI Act obligation to the right operator (provider/deployer/importer/distributor) and recall the seven high-risk provider obligations - plus that existing law (GDPR, non-discrimination, product liability, IP) still applies.
lessons.body:
## Same system, different hats

The AIGP tests role allocation harder than almost anything else in Domain II. The EU AI Act assigns obligations to **operators**, and the two you must never confuse are:

- **Provider (Article 3(3))** - *'a natural or legal person ... that develops an AI system ... or that has [one] developed and places it on the market or puts it into service under its own name or trademark'*. Informally: the **developer/maker**. Carries the lion's share of high-risk obligations.
- **Deployer (Article 3(4))** - *'a natural or legal person ... using an AI system under its authority'* in a professional capacity. Informally: the **user/operator organisation**. Carries a smaller, use-time set of obligations.

Two more operators appear in Annex-role questions: the **importer** (places a third-country provider's system on the EU market) and the **distributor** (makes it available in the supply chain). The BoK explicitly says to *'understand the differences in requirements based on organizational context (e.g., providers, deployers, importers, and distributors)'* - so expect a stem that hinges on which hat the org is wearing.

> **Exam tip - the role flip:** if you build a model in-house and put it into service, you may be **both** provider and deployer. And a deployer can be **re-classified as a provider** if it substantially modifies a high-risk system or puts its own name on it, or repurposes another system into a high-risk use. Watch for stems that quietly flip the role.

## The high-risk obligation set (who owns what)

For a high-risk system, the **provider** must build and document the following before it goes on the market. Learn the seven headline obligations and their article numbers:

| # | Obligation | Article | In one line |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk management system** | 9 | A *continuous, iterative* process across the whole lifecycle - identify, estimate, evaluate, mitigate risks. |
| 2 | **Data and data governance** | 10 | Training/validation/testing data must be relevant, **sufficiently representative**, and to the best extent possible free of errors; examine for **bias**. |
| 3 | **Technical documentation** | 11 | Draw up docs proving conformity (kept up to date), the basis for the conformity assessment. |
| 4 | **Record-keeping / logging** | 12 | The system must **automatically record events (logs)** over its lifetime for traceability and post-market monitoring. |
| 5 | **Transparency & information to deployers** | 13 | Provide clear **instructions for use** so deployers can understand and operate the system correctly. |
| 6 | **Human oversight** | 14 | Design so people can **effectively oversee** it - understand it, catch automation bias, override, and **stop** it. |
| 7 | **Accuracy, robustness & cybersecurity** | 15 | Appropriate, consistent performance; resilience to errors and to attacks (data/model poisoning, adversarial examples). |

Wrapping these: the provider must run a **conformity assessment**, register the system in the EU database, affix the **CE marking**, and put a **quality management system** in place.

> **Exam tip - representative, not perfect:** Article 10 says data should be *'relevant, sufficiently representative, and to the best extent possible, free of errors and complete'*. The Act does **not** demand perfect, error-free data - an option claiming '100% error-free data is required' is wrong. 'Sufficiently representative' is the phrase to remember.

## What the *deployer* must do

Deployers are not off the hook - they have a distinct, lighter set, including:

- **Use the system in accordance with the provider's instructions for use.**
- **Assign human oversight** to competent, trained, resourced people (they operationalise Article 14 on the ground).
- **Monitor** operation and **inform the provider/authorities** of risks or serious incidents.
- **Keep the logs** the system generates (where under their control).
- For certain Annex III systems, carry out a **fundamental rights impact assessment (FRIA, Article 27)** before first use - notably public bodies and providers of essential services.
- Meet any **Article 50 transparency** duties they trigger (e.g. disclosing a deepfake, informing people subject to emotion recognition).

> **Exam tip - human oversight is shared:** the **provider designs** the oversight *capability* (the stop button, the interface) under Article 14; the **deployer assigns** the actual humans to use it. A question asking 'whose obligation is human oversight?' wants you to see it's **both**, split by design-time vs use-time.

## Tier 3 in detail - Article 50 transparency

The limited-risk duties, and note they mostly fall on **providers for generation** and **deployers for use**:

- **Chatbots / direct interaction** - the **provider** designs the system so people are informed they are interacting with an **AI system**, unless it is obvious.
- **Synthetic content marking** - the **provider** of a generative system marks outputs (audio/image/video/text) as **artificially generated or manipulated**, in a machine-readable way where feasible.
- **Deepfakes** - the **deployer** who generates or manipulates image/audio/video that resembles real people/events **discloses** it is artificially generated (with narrow artistic/satirical carve-outs).
- **Emotion recognition / biometric categorisation** - the **deployer** informs the natural persons exposed.
- **AI-generated public-interest text** - the **deployer** discloses when published text on matters of public interest is AI-generated (unless human-reviewed with editorial responsibility).

## Existing laws still apply (II.A and II.B)

The EU AI Act sits **on top of** law that already governs AI - it does not replace it. The BoK devotes two whole sub-domains to this:

- **II.A - data-privacy law** (GDPR): notice, choice, consent, **purpose limitation**, **data minimisation**, **privacy by design**, controller obligations (**DPIAs**, third-party processors, **cross-border transfers**, data-subject rights, breach notification, record-keeping), and special-category data (**biometrics**). Full treatment in Lesson 4.
- **II.B - other existing laws:** **non-discrimination** law (employment, credit, lending, housing, insurance), **consumer protection** (unfair/deceptive practices), **product liability** (design/manufacturing defects), and **intellectual property** (limits on using data for training).

> **Exam tip - 'which law?':** a discriminatory hiring model can breach **non-discrimination law**, the **EU AI Act** (high-risk employment), **and** GDPR (if it profiles personal data) all at once. When a stem asks which regime applies, the answer is often 'more than one' - and the *best* option names the one the facts most directly trigger.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Under the EU AI Act, the operator that DEVELOPS a high-risk AI system and places it on the market under its own name is the:

options.text: Provider
Deployer
Importer
Distributor

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Developing a system and placing it on the market under your own name/trademark is the **provider** role (Article 3(3)), which carries the bulk of high-risk obligations. A **deployer** uses a system under its authority; an **importer** brings a third-country provider's system into the EU; a **distributor** makes it available downstream.

knowledgeCheck.stem: For a high-risk AI system, who is responsible for human oversight under the EU AI Act?

options.text: Both - the provider designs the oversight capability (Article 14), and the deployer assigns competent people to exercise it
Only the provider - oversight is purely a design-time obligation
Only the deployer - the provider has no oversight duties
Neither - human oversight is optional for high-risk systems

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Human oversight is **split**: the **provider** must design the system so it *can* be effectively overseen (interface, stop button) per Article 14; the **deployer** must **assign** trained, resourced humans to actually oversee it in use. It's not one-sided, and it is mandatory for high-risk systems, not optional.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Article 10 (data governance) requires that training, validation and testing data for a high-risk system be:

options.text: Relevant, sufficiently representative, and to the best extent possible free of errors and complete
Guaranteed 100% error-free and perfectly complete
Sourced exclusively from within the EU
Fully anonymised in every case, with no exceptions

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Act's standard is **'relevant, sufficiently representative, and to the best extent possible, free of errors and complete'** - a reasonableness bar, not perfection. It does not mandate literally error-free data, EU-only sourcing, or universal anonymisation (indeed Article 10 permits limited processing of special-category data for bias detection under safeguards).

lessons.title: General-purpose AI duties and the implementation timeline
lessons.objective: Explain GPAI model provider obligations (Article 53), the systemic-risk extras (Articles 51 & 55, incl. the 10^25 FLOP presumption), and match each obligation to its date on the phased timeline.
lessons.body:
## A separate track for foundation models

Large models that can do many things - the foundation models behind chatbots, copilots and image generators - didn't fit the four-tier, use-case pyramid, so the EU AI Act gives them their own regime: **general-purpose AI (GPAI) models**, Articles 51-56.

- **General-purpose AI model (Article 3(63))** - a model that *'displays significant generality'* and *'is capable of ... performing a wide range of distinct tasks'* and can be integrated into downstream systems. (A model, not yet an application.)
- **General-purpose AI system (Article 3(66))** - an AI **system** built on a GPAI model that can serve many purposes.

Crucially, the tier a *downstream* application lands in (Lesson 1) is **independent** of the GPAI rules. A hospital that builds a high-risk diagnostic tool on top of a general-purpose model is a **deployer/provider of a high-risk system**; the model maker is a **GPAI model provider**. Different duties, same supply chain.

> **Exam tip - don't conflate the two:** 'a GPAI model provider' and 'a high-risk-system deployer' are different roles with different obligations. A stem that describes building an app on a foundation model is inviting you to keep them apart.

## Baseline GPAI provider duties (Article 53)

Every GPAI model provider must:

1. **Draw up and keep technical documentation** of the model (training, testing, evaluation), available to the AI Office and national authorities on request (Annex XI).
2. **Provide information and documentation to downstream providers** who integrate the model - enough to *'understand the capabilities and limitations'* and meet their own obligations (Annex XII).
3. **Put in place a policy to comply with EU copyright law**, including respecting rights reservations (text-and-data-mining opt-outs).
4. **Publish a sufficiently detailed summary of the content used for training**, using the AI Office's template.

> **Exam tip - the open-source carve-out (and its limit):** free and open-source GPAI models are **exempt from (1) and (2)** - *except* where the model has **systemic risk**. The copyright policy and training-data summary duties are **not** waived by open-sourcing. So 'open-source models have no GPAI obligations at all' is wrong.

## GPAI with systemic risk (Articles 51 & 55)

The most capable models get an extra layer. A GPAI model is designated **systemic-risk** when it has **high-impact capabilities** - and there is a **presumption** of that when *'the cumulative amount of computation used for its training measured in floating point operations is greater than 10^25'* (Article 51). The Commission can also designate a model by decision.

On top of the Article 53 baseline, systemic-risk providers must (Article 55):

- **Perform model evaluation, including adversarial testing (red-teaming)**, to identify and mitigate systemic risks.
- **Assess and mitigate systemic risks at Union level.**
- **Track, document and report serious incidents** to the AI Office (and national authorities) **without undue delay**, with corrective measures.
- **Ensure an adequate level of cybersecurity** for the model and its physical infrastructure.

> **Exam tip:** the **10^25 FLOP** threshold and **red-teaming/adversarial testing** are the two most quotable systemic-risk facts. Note it is a *presumption* (rebuttable / adjustable by delegated act), not an eternal hard line.

## The implementation timeline (currency-critical)

The Act entered into force but its obligations **phase in**. 'Everything applies from day one' is a top distractor. The **structure** below is the durable, examinable fact - prohibitions first, then GPAI, then high-risk - and it did not change even though the two high-risk dates were later deferred (see the currency note):

| Date | What starts to apply |
|---|---|
| **1 Aug 2024** | **Entry into force.** No requirements apply yet. |
| **2 Feb 2025** | **Prohibitions (Article 5)** and **AI-literacy** obligations start. |
| **2 May 2025** | Codes of practice to be ready. |
| **2 Aug 2025** | **GPAI model** obligations, **governance** bodies, notified bodies, confidentiality and **penalties** apply. (GPAI models already on the market before this date have until **2 Aug 2027** to comply.) |
| **2 Dec 2027** | **High-risk obligations for Annex III** use-case (stand-alone) systems apply, together with Article 50 transparency and the bulk of the remaining obligations. *(Deferred from the original 2 Aug 2026 by the Digital Omnibus - see the currency note.)* |
| **2 Aug 2028** | **Article 6(1) / Annex I** high-risk (product-safety-embedded) obligations apply. *(Deferred from the original 2 Aug 2027 by the Digital Omnibus.)* |

> **Currency note (as of 2 July 2026):** the two **high-risk** application dates were **deferred by the Digital Omnibus on AI** - provisional political agreement 6-7 May 2026, confirmed by the Council 13 May 2026, endorsed by the European Parliament 16 June 2026, with publication in the Official Journal expected shortly (before the original 2 Aug 2026 deadline). **Stand-alone Annex III high-risk** moved from **2 Aug 2026 → 2 December 2027**, and **product-embedded Annex I high-risk** moved from **2 Aug 2027 → 2 August 2028**. **Prohibitions (2 Feb 2025)** and **GPAI (2 Aug 2025)** are **unchanged**. Re-verify the final dates against EUR-Lex once the Omnibus is published in the Official Journal.

> **Exam tip - anchor the dates that did NOT move:** **prohibitions Feb 2025** and **GPAI Aug 2025** are settled and unchanged - lean on them. If a scenario says 'today is early 2026', prohibitions and GPAI duties are **live**, but **no** high-risk obligations are yet in force (the earliest, Annex III, is now 2 Dec 2027). Matching an obligation to its phase is fair game; because the high-risk calendar dates were deferred, the exam-safe move is to reason from the **order** (prohibitions → GPAI → Annex III high-risk → Annex I high-risk) rather than memorising a single moving date.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Which is an obligation on EVERY provider of a general-purpose AI model under Article 53 (even absent systemic risk)?

options.text: Publish a sufficiently detailed summary of the content used to train the model
Conduct adversarial red-team testing for systemic risk
Report serious incidents to the AI Office without undue delay
Register the model as a high-risk system in the EU database

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The **training-content summary** (plus technical docs, downstream information, and an EU-copyright policy) is a **baseline Article 53** duty for all GPAI providers. Adversarial red-teaming and serious-incident reporting are **Article 55** duties reserved for **systemic-risk** models. Registering as high-risk confuses the separate high-risk regime with the GPAI track.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Under Article 51, a general-purpose AI model is PRESUMED to have systemic risk when the cumulative training compute exceeds:

options.text: 10^25 floating point operations (FLOP)
10^25 model parameters
10^12 floating point operations (FLOP)
There is no quantitative threshold - it is purely a Commission decision

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The presumption trigger is **>10^25 FLOP of cumulative training compute**. It is measured in **FLOP (computation)**, not parameters; the exponent is 25, not 12; and while the Commission *can* also designate a model by decision, there **is** a published quantitative presumption.

knowledgeCheck.stem: On the EU AI Act timeline, which obligations were the FIRST to start applying after entry into force?

options.text: The prohibitions on unacceptable-risk practices (and AI-literacy duties), from 2 February 2025
The full high-risk obligations for Annex III systems, immediately on entry into force in August 2024
The Annex I product-safety high-risk obligations, from February 2025
All obligations applied simultaneously on the date of entry into force

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The **Article 5 prohibitions and AI-literacy** duties came first, on **2 Feb 2025** - six months after entry into force. High-risk **Annex III** obligations don't apply until **2 December 2027** and **Annex I** high-risk until **2 August 2028** (both deferred from the original Aug 2026 / Aug 2027 by the Digital Omnibus, agreed May 2026), and the whole point of the phased timeline is that obligations do **not** all start at once. Note the *order* is what this question tests - prohibitions are unambiguously first regardless of where the later high-risk dates land.

lessons.title: NIST AI RMF, ISO standards, OECD principles - and GDPR x AI
lessons.objective: Place each instrument on the binding/voluntary/certifiable spectrum, recall NIST's four core functions (and ARIA/Playbook), distinguish ISO/IEC 42001 (management system) from 22989 (terminology) and the OECD principles, and identify when GDPR obligations (DPIA, lawful basis, transfers) are triggered by AI.
lessons.body:
## Two questions the exam keeps asking

Domain II's non-EU-AI-Act half turns on two habits of mind:

1. **Is this instrument binding, voluntary, or certifiable?** Get this reflex right and a third of the framework questions answer themselves.
2. **Does personal data touch this AI?** If yes, **GDPR applies** - full stop - regardless of what the AI Act does.

## Binding vs voluntary vs certifiable

| Instrument | Nature | What it is |
|---|---|---|
| **EU AI Act** | **Binding law** | Enforceable regulation with penalties; the only *mandatory* item here. |
| **GDPR** | **Binding law** | Enforceable data-protection regulation; governs personal data in AI. |
| **NIST AI RMF 1.0** | **Voluntary framework** | US guidance for managing AI risk; *'intended for voluntary use'*. |
| **OECD AI Principles** | **Voluntary (soft law)** | Intergovernmental principles; not directly enforceable. |
| **ISO/IEC 42001** | **Certifiable standard** | A management-system standard an organisation can be **audited and certified** against. |
| **ISO/IEC 22989** | **Terminology standard** | Establishes AI **vocabulary and concepts**; nothing to certify. |

> **Exam tip:** the two most-tested confusions are (a) thinking **NIST AI RMF is mandatory** - it is voluntary; and (b) thinking **ISO/IEC 42001 certifies a model** - it certifies a **management system** (process), not any single AI model or its accuracy.

## NIST AI RMF 1.0 - the four core functions

Released **26 January 2023** by the US National Institute of Standards and Technology, the AI RMF is a **voluntary** framework organised around four **core functions** (each broken into categories and subcategories). Learn all four:

- **GOVERN** - a **cross-cutting** function that *'cultivates and implements a culture of risk management'* and is *'infused throughout AI risk management'*. Policies, roles, accountability, culture. (This is the one that runs through the other three.)
- **MAP** - *'establishes the context to frame risks related to an AI system'* and identifies risks and contributing factors. (Context and problem-framing.)
- **MEASURE** - uses *'quantitative, qualitative, or mixed-method tools ... to analyze, assess, benchmark, and monitor AI risk'*. (Metrics and testing.)
- **MANAGE** - *'allocat[es] risk resources to mapped and measured risks'* and plans responses, recovery and communication about incidents. (Acting on and prioritising risk.)

Supporting pieces the BoK names:
- **The AI RMF Playbook** - a companion offering suggested, actionable ways to implement the core functions' subcategories.
- **The Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1, July 2024)** - a cross-sectoral profile for GenAI-specific risks.
- **The ARIA program (Assessing Risks and Impacts of AI)** - a NIST **evaluation/measurement** program developing methodologies, tools and metrics for AI safety and real-world impact. The BoK explicitly lists 'the NIST ARIA program for methodologies, tools, metrics and measurements on AI safety'.

> **Exam tip - GOVERN is the odd one out:** Map, Measure and Manage are broadly sequential; **GOVERN is cross-cutting** and wraps all three. If asked 'which NIST function is present throughout / is about culture and accountability?', it's **GOVERN**.

## ISO/IEC 42001 and 22989

The BoK, in II.D, tells you to *'understand the core ISO AI standards (i.e., 22989 and 42001)'*. Keep their jobs distinct:

- **ISO/IEC 42001** - the **AI management system (AIMS)** standard (2023). It specifies requirements to **establish, implement, maintain and continually improve** an AI management system, in the same certifiable, Plan-Do-Check-Act family as ISO 9001 (quality) and ISO/IEC 27001 (information security). You certify an **organisation's processes** for governing AI - not a model. This is the framework you'd point a regulated buyer at when they ask *'can you prove you manage AI responsibly?'*
- **ISO/IEC 22989** - **AI concepts and terminology.** It establishes the **vocabulary** the field uses (what 'AI system', 'machine learning', 'training data' etc. mean). It's a reference standard, not something you certify against.

> **Exam tip:** map the need to the standard. *'We want to be audited/certified for responsible AI governance'* -> **42001**. *'We need a shared definition of AI terms'* -> **22989**. *'We want a voluntary process to manage AI risk'* -> **NIST AI RMF**. *'We must legally conform'* -> **EU AI Act**.

## OECD AI Principles

Adopted in **2019** and **updated in 2024**, the OECD AI Principles are the **first intergovernmental standard on AI** and the basis the **G20** drew its principles from. The **five value-based principles**:

1. **Inclusive growth, sustainable development and well-being**
2. **Human rights and democratic values, including fairness and privacy**
3. **Transparency and explainability**
4. **Robustness, security and safety**
5. **Accountability**

They are **soft law** - influential, widely adopted (dozens of adherents), but not directly enforceable. Recognise them as *principles/values*, distinct from the operational NIST functions and the certifiable ISO standards.

## GDPR x AI (sub-domain II.A)

GDPR governs **personal data** whenever it enters an AI system - in a prompt, a training set, an output, or a log. The AIGP tests the specific triggers:

- **Lawful basis and purpose limitation** - you need a valid basis to process personal data, and you can't quietly repurpose data collected for X to train a model for Y.
- **Data minimisation & privacy by design** - collect only what's needed; bake protection in (echoes Article 10's discipline but is a *separate* GDPR duty).
- **DPIA (data-protection impact assessment)** - required for processing **likely to result in a high risk** to individuals' rights, which AI profiling/large-scale or sensitive-data use routinely triggers. *(Distinguish from the AI Act's FRIA in Article 27 - a related but separate assessment.)*
- **Special-category data** - biometrics, health, etc. carry heightened conditions; the BoK's II.A calls out **biometrics** specifically.
- **Automated decision-making (Article 22 GDPR)** - individuals have rights around solely-automated decisions with legal/similarly-significant effects (information, human intervention, contest).
- **Third-party processors** - a controller using a vendor/model API must have the right contracts and safeguards.
- **Cross-border transfers / data residency** - moving personal data outside the EEA needs a lawful transfer mechanism; where prompts/logs are stored matters.
- **Retention, logging and breach notification** - keep personal data no longer than necessary; the same logs the AI Act wants for traceability are **personal data** if they contain identifiers, and a leak may be a notifiable **breach**.

> **Exam tip - PII in prompts:** typing a customer's personal data into a third-party LLM is a **GDPR processing event** - it needs a lawful basis, a processor agreement, minimisation, and attention to where the data (and any logs) reside. The AI Act tier is a *separate* question. Where a scenario is fundamentally about personal data, the answer lives in **GDPR**, even if the AI Act also applies.

## Putting the frameworks together

For one deployment you might: **conform** to the EU AI Act (binding), **comply** with GDPR (binding) for the personal data, **run** your governance using the NIST AI RMF (voluntary process), **certify** your management system to ISO/IEC 42001 (certifiable), speak a common vocabulary via ISO/IEC 22989, and align to the OECD principles (values). Different instruments, different jobs - which is exactly what Domain II asks you to sort.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Which statement correctly characterises these instruments?

options.text: The EU AI Act is binding law, the NIST AI RMF is voluntary, and ISO/IEC 42001 is a certifiable management-system standard
The NIST AI RMF is legally mandatory in the EU
ISO/IEC 42001 certifies that an individual AI model is accurate and unbiased
The OECD AI Principles are directly enforceable regulations with penalties

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The EU AI Act is **binding**, the NIST AI RMF is **voluntary**, and ISO/IEC 42001 is a **certifiable management-system** standard - all correct. NIST's framework is not EU law; ISO/IEC 42001 certifies an organisation's **management system (process)**, not a single model's accuracy; and the OECD principles are **soft law**, not enforceable regulations.

knowledgeCheck.stem: In the NIST AI RMF, which core function is CROSS-CUTTING - a culture of risk management infused throughout the other three?

options.text: GOVERN
MAP
MEASURE
MANAGE

knowledgeCheck.explanation: **GOVERN** is the cross-cutting function - it cultivates the risk-management culture, policies and accountability that run through **MAP** (frame context/identify risks), **MEASURE** (analyse/benchmark risks) and **MANAGE** (act on and prioritise risks).

knowledgeCheck.stem: A support team wants to paste customers' personal data into a third-party LLM to draft replies. Which legal regime most directly governs that personal data?

options.text: GDPR - it applies to processing personal data (lawful basis, minimisation, processor agreement, transfers) regardless of the AI Act tier
Only the EU AI Act - GDPR stops applying once data is inside an AI system
ISO/IEC 22989, because it defines AI terminology
The NIST AI RMF, because it is mandatory for personal-data processing

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Personal data in a prompt is a **GDPR processing event** - lawful basis, purpose limitation, minimisation, a processor agreement, and cross-border-transfer rules all apply, independent of any AI Act classification. GDPR does not switch off inside AI; ISO/IEC 22989 is a terminology standard (not a legal regime); and the NIST AI RMF is voluntary, not mandatory.

questions.stem: The EU AI Act sorts AI systems into how many principal risk tiers, and what are they?

options.text: Four: unacceptable (prohibited), high, limited (transparency), and minimal
Two: legal and illegal
Three: low, medium and high
Five: banned, critical, high, medium and low

questions.explanation: The Act uses a **four-tier** risk pyramid: **unacceptable/prohibited, high, limited (transparency), and minimal/none**. It is not a binary legal/illegal split, not a generic low/medium/high scale, and not five tiers.

questions.stem: Under the EU AI Act, which term describes the organisation that DEVELOPS an AI system and puts it on the market under its own name?

options.text: Provider
Deployer
Data subject
Supervisory authority

questions.explanation: The **provider** (Article 3(3)) develops the system and places it on the market under its own name/trademark. A **deployer** merely uses it; a **data subject** is an individual whose data is processed (a GDPR term); a **supervisory authority** is a regulator.

questions.stem: How many core functions does the NIST AI Risk Management Framework define?

options.text: Four - Govern, Map, Measure and Manage
Three - Plan, Do and Check
Five - Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond and Recover
Two - Assess and Mitigate

questions.explanation: The AI RMF has **four** core functions: **Govern, Map, Measure, Manage**. Plan-Do-Check is a management-system cycle; Identify/Protect/Detect/Respond/Recover is the NIST *Cybersecurity* Framework, a different NIST framework.

questions.stem: Is compliance with the NIST AI Risk Management Framework legally mandatory?

options.text: No - it is a voluntary framework
Yes - it is mandatory for all US companies
Yes - it is mandatory throughout the EU
Yes - it is mandatory for any company using AI worldwide

questions.explanation: The NIST AI RMF is **voluntary** - NIST describes it as intended for voluntary use. It is not a mandate in the US, the EU, or globally; that binding role belongs to laws like the EU AI Act and GDPR.

questions.stem: ISO/IEC 42001 is best described as a standard for:

options.text: An AI management system (AIMS) - a certifiable set of organisational processes for governing AI
Certifying that a specific AI model is accurate and free of bias
The vocabulary and concepts used in AI
A binding EU regulation with fines for non-compliance

questions.explanation: ISO/IEC 42001 specifies an **AI management system** - certifiable organisational **processes**, in the ISO management-system family. It does not certify a single model's accuracy, is not a terminology standard (that's **22989**), and is a voluntary standard, not binding EU law.

questions.stem: ISO/IEC 22989 primarily provides:

options.text: AI concepts and terminology (a shared vocabulary)
A certifiable AI management system
The EU AI Act's list of prohibited practices
NIST's Generative AI Profile

questions.explanation: ISO/IEC 22989 establishes AI **concepts and terminology**. The certifiable management system is **ISO/IEC 42001**; the prohibited-practice list is EU AI Act Article 5; the Generative AI Profile (AI 600-1) is a NIST document.

questions.stem: The OECD AI Principles are best characterised as:

options.text: Voluntary intergovernmental principles for trustworthy AI (soft law), first adopted in 2019 and updated in 2024
A binding treaty enforceable in national courts
A certifiable technical standard like ISO/IEC 42001
A section of the EU AI Act

questions.explanation: The OECD AI Principles are **voluntary, intergovernmental soft law** (2019, updated 2024) - the first intergovernmental AI standard. They are not a binding treaty, not a certifiable standard, and not part of the EU AI Act.

questions.stem: Which assessment is the GDPR instrument for processing likely to result in a high risk to individuals' rights?

options.text: A Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)
A conformity assessment under the EU AI Act
A Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (FRIA)
A NIST ARIA evaluation

questions.explanation: The **DPIA** is the GDPR mechanism for high-risk processing of personal data. A **conformity assessment** is the EU AI Act's high-risk pre-market check; a **FRIA** is the AI Act's Article 27 assessment (related but distinct); **ARIA** is a NIST evaluation program.

questions.stem: A company builds an AI system that assigns each citizen a 'trustworthiness score' from their everyday social behaviour, used by public bodies to deny access to services. Under the EU AI Act this is:

options.text: A prohibited practice (social scoring) under Article 5
A permitted high-risk system if a conformity assessment is passed
A limited-risk system requiring only a transparency notice
Outside the scope of the EU AI Act

questions.explanation: General-purpose **social scoring** leading to unjustified detrimental treatment is **prohibited** under Article 5 - no conformity assessment legalises it. It is therefore not a permitted high-risk system, not a mere transparency case, and squarely within the Act's scope.

questions.stem: A bank deploys a third-party AI system to decide consumer loan applications. Which EU AI Act tier applies, and why?

options.text: High-risk - creditworthiness/access to essential services is an Annex III use case
Unacceptable - all automated credit decisions are prohibited
Limited-risk - it only needs to disclose that AI is used
Minimal-risk - financial services are excluded from the Act

questions.explanation: AI used for **creditworthiness / access to essential private services** is an **Annex III high-risk** use case. Automated credit decisions are not banned, transparency-only is the wrong tier, and financial-services AI is not excluded.

questions.stem: For a high-risk AI system, which obligation requires the system to AUTOMATICALLY record events (logs) over its lifetime?

options.text: Record-keeping / logging (Article 12)
Human oversight (Article 14)
Accuracy and robustness (Article 15)
Data governance (Article 10)

questions.explanation: **Article 12 (record-keeping)** requires automatic event logging over the system's lifetime for traceability and post-market monitoring. Article 14 is human oversight, Article 15 is accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity, and Article 10 is data governance - related duties, but not the logging one.

questions.stem: A hospital fine-tunes an off-the-shelf general-purpose model into a tool that diagnoses disease from scans, then puts it into service under its own name. Which best describes the hospital's role for that diagnostic tool?

options.text: It becomes a provider of a high-risk AI system (and must meet the high-risk obligations)
It is only a deployer, with no provider obligations, because it started from someone else's model
It is a GPAI model provider governed solely by Article 53
It has no obligations because diagnosis is a purely clinical activity

questions.explanation: By substantially adapting the model into a high-risk medical tool and putting it into service under its own name, the hospital takes on the **provider** role for a **high-risk system** - full obligations. Starting from a third-party model doesn't limit it to deployer status, the GPAI-provider track governs the *model maker*, and medical AI is not exempt.

questions.stem: Which of these is a LIMITED-RISK transparency obligation under Article 50 rather than a high-risk obligation?

options.text: Ensuring users are informed when they are interacting with a chatbot (unless obvious)
Establishing a lifecycle risk-management system
Drawing up technical documentation for conformity assessment
Designing the system for effective human oversight

questions.explanation: Informing users they're dealing with an **AI system/chatbot** is an **Article 50 transparency** duty (limited-risk). Risk-management systems, technical documentation, and human-oversight design are **high-risk** obligations (Articles 9, 11, 14).

questions.stem: A provider of a generative AI system that outputs images and audio must, under Article 50:

options.text: Mark the outputs as artificially generated or manipulated in a machine-readable format (where technically feasible)
Obtain a CE marking for the generated images
Delete all generated content after 30 days
Register each generated image in the EU high-risk database

questions.explanation: Article 50 requires providers of generative systems to **mark synthetic outputs as artificially generated/manipulated** in a machine-readable way where feasible. CE marking and the EU database belong to the **high-risk** regime, and there is no blanket 30-day deletion rule.

questions.stem: Which is a baseline obligation for a provider of a general-purpose AI model, even without systemic risk (Article 53)?

options.text: Put in place a policy to comply with EU copyright law
Conduct Union-level systemic-risk assessment and mitigation
Ensure cybersecurity of the model's physical infrastructure as a systemic-risk measure
Report serious incidents to the AI Office without undue delay

questions.explanation: An **EU-copyright-compliance policy** (alongside technical docs, downstream information, and a training-content summary) is a **baseline Article 53** duty. Union-level systemic-risk assessment, infrastructure cybersecurity, and serious-incident reporting are **Article 55** duties for **systemic-risk** models only.

questions.stem: A model is presumed to be a general-purpose AI model 'with systemic risk'. Which ADDITIONAL obligation does that trigger under Article 55?

options.text: Performing and documenting adversarial testing (red-teaming) to identify and mitigate systemic risks
Publishing a summary of training data (this is unique to systemic-risk models)
Providing instructions for use to consumers
Affixing a CE marking to the model

questions.explanation: **Article 55** adds **model evaluation including adversarial red-teaming**, Union-level risk mitigation, serious-incident reporting, and cybersecurity. The training-data summary is a **baseline Article 53** duty (not unique to systemic-risk), consumer instructions/CE marking belong to the high-risk-system regime, not the GPAI track.

questions.stem: It is early 2026. Which EU AI Act obligations are ALREADY in force?

options.text: The Article 5 prohibitions (since Feb 2025) and GPAI model obligations (since Aug 2025)
The full high-risk obligations for Annex III systems
The Annex I product-safety high-risk obligations
No obligations are in force yet - nothing applies until the high-risk obligations land

questions.explanation: By early 2026 the **prohibitions** (2 Feb 2025) and **GPAI** duties (2 Aug 2025) are **live** - and these two dates are settled/unchanged. The high-risk obligations are **not** yet in force: **Annex III** starts **2 December 2027** and **Annex I** **2 August 2028** (both deferred from the original Aug 2026 / Aug 2027 by the Digital Omnibus, agreed May 2026). So it is wrong to say *nothing* applies. The answer holds for any 'early 2026' scenario regardless of the exact high-risk dates, because prohibitions and GPAI are the early anchors.

questions.stem: Which NIST AI RMF function 'establishes the context to frame risks related to an AI system' and identifies risks?

options.text: MAP
GOVERN
MEASURE
MANAGE

questions.explanation: **MAP** establishes context and identifies risks and contributing factors. **GOVERN** is the cross-cutting culture/accountability function, **MEASURE** analyses and benchmarks risks, and **MANAGE** allocates resources to and acts on them.

questions.stem: An organisation wants an independent, auditable certification that its processes for governing AI meet a recognised standard. Which instrument fits?

options.text: ISO/IEC 42001 (AI management system certification)
The NIST AI RMF, which issues certifications
The OECD AI Principles, which can be certified against
ISO/IEC 22989, which certifies AI governance

questions.explanation: **ISO/IEC 42001** is the certifiable **AI management system** standard - the right answer for auditable certification of processes. The **NIST AI RMF** and **OECD principles** are voluntary and not certification schemes, and **22989** is a terminology standard, not a certifiable management system.

questions.stem: A marketing team wants to upload a spreadsheet of named customers to a public generative-AI tool to segment them. Which is the MOST relevant obligation to raise first?

options.text: GDPR - there must be a lawful basis, data minimisation, a processor arrangement, and attention to where data/logs reside
Article 5 of the EU AI Act, because customer segmentation is prohibited
ISO/IEC 22989, to define the term 'customer'
Nothing - once data is in an AI tool it is no longer personal data

questions.explanation: Uploading named customers is **personal-data processing**, so **GDPR** (lawful basis, minimisation, processor terms, transfers/residency) is the first flag. Segmentation isn't an Article 5 prohibition, a terminology standard doesn't govern the data, and data doesn't stop being personal by entering an AI tool.

questions.stem: Under the EU AI Act, a deployer of a high-risk AI system is typically required to:

options.text: Use the system per the provider's instructions, assign competent human oversight, and monitor operation
Draw up the technical documentation and run the conformity assessment
Affix the CE marking to the system
Publish the training-data summary for the underlying model

questions.explanation: **Deployers** must follow the instructions for use, **assign human oversight**, and **monitor** the system (informing the provider/authorities of incidents). Technical documentation, conformity assessment, and CE marking are **provider** duties, and the training-data summary is a **GPAI provider** duty.

questions.stem: The NIST 'ARIA' program is best described as:

options.text: A NIST program developing methodologies, tools, metrics and measurements for AI safety and real-world impact
A binding US law regulating high-risk AI
The EU AI Act's conformity-assessment procedure
An ISO certification for AI management systems

questions.explanation: **ARIA (Assessing Risks and Impacts of AI)** is a NIST **evaluation/measurement** program for AI safety and impact, named in BoK II.D. It is not a US law, not the EU AI Act's conformity assessment, and not an ISO certification.

questions.stem: A start-up releases a general-purpose AI model under a genuine free and open-source licence. The model is NOT designated systemic-risk. Which Article 53 duties still apply?

options.text: The EU-copyright-compliance policy and the public training-content summary still apply; the technical-documentation and downstream-information duties are exempted
All Article 53 duties are waived because the model is open-source
No duties apply unless the model is later designated systemic-risk
Only the technical-documentation duty applies; the copyright and training-summary duties are waived

questions.explanation: The open-source exemption covers the **technical-documentation and downstream-information** duties, but the **copyright policy** and **training-content summary** are **not** waived. So it's wrong that everything is waived, that nothing applies, or that documentation is the surviving duty (it's the opposite).

questions.stem: An AI tool sits in an Annex III area (employment) but only performs a narrow procedural task and does no profiling. What does the Article 6(3) filter imply?

options.text: It may be treated as NOT high-risk - but the provider must document that assessment, and any profiling would keep it high-risk
It is automatically prohibited because it touches employment
It is automatically high-risk with no exceptions because it is in Annex III
It is exempt from the entire EU AI Act, including transparency duties

questions.explanation: Article 6(3) lets a narrow-task, non-profiling Annex III system be treated as **not high-risk**, provided the provider **documents** the assessment - and **profiling always makes it high-risk**. It isn't prohibited (that's Article 5), isn't automatically high-risk (that's the point of the filter), and isn't wholly exempt from the Act (e.g. transparency can still apply).

questions.stem: A recruitment AI systematically rejects candidates over 50 at a higher rate. An AIGP is asked which legal exposures apply. Which is the MOST complete answer?

options.text: Potentially all three: EU AI Act (high-risk employment obligations), non-discrimination law (age), and GDPR (profiling of personal data)
Only the EU AI Act applies; discrimination and data-protection law are superseded by it
Only non-discrimination law applies; the EU AI Act does not cover employment
No law applies because the bias was unintentional

questions.explanation: The scenario can trigger the **EU AI Act** (Annex III employment high-risk), **non-discrimination** law (age), **and GDPR** (profiling personal data) simultaneously - the AI Act does not supersede existing law (BoK II.A/II.B exist precisely because it stacks). Employment *is* covered by the Act, and lack of intent does not remove discrimination or data-protection liability.

questions.stem: Which pairing of governance need to instrument is correct?

options.text: 'We must legally conform in the EU' -> EU AI Act; 'we want a voluntary process to manage AI risk' -> NIST AI RMF; 'we want to be certified' -> ISO/IEC 42001
'We must legally conform in the EU' -> NIST AI RMF; 'we want certification' -> OECD principles
'We want a shared AI vocabulary' -> ISO/IEC 42001; 'we want a management system' -> ISO/IEC 22989
'We want to prohibit social scoring' -> the OECD principles as binding law

questions.explanation: The correct mapping is **EU AI Act = binding conformity**, **NIST AI RMF = voluntary process**, **ISO/IEC 42001 = certification**. Option B mislabels NIST/OECD; option C swaps 42001 and 22989 (42001=management system, 22989=vocabulary); and the OECD principles are soft law, not the binding source of the social-scoring ban (that's EU AI Act Article 5).

questions.stem: Under the EU AI Act, how do human-oversight responsibilities divide between provider and deployer for a high-risk system? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: The provider must design the system so it can be effectively overseen (interface, ability to intervene/stop) - Article 14
The deployer must assign competent, trained, resourced natural persons to carry out the oversight
Only the provider has any human-oversight responsibility; the deployer has none
Human oversight is optional for high-risk systems if accuracy is high enough

questions.explanation: Human oversight is **shared**: the **provider designs** the oversight capability (Article 14) and the **deployer assigns** the humans who use it. So A and B are both correct; it is not provider-only (C), and it is **mandatory** for high-risk systems regardless of accuracy (D).

questions.stem: Which statements about the binding-versus-voluntary nature of AI governance instruments are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: The EU AI Act and GDPR are binding law with enforcement/penalties
The NIST AI RMF and the OECD AI Principles are voluntary
ISO/IEC 42001 is a standard an organisation can be certified against
ISO/IEC 42001 is a binding EU regulation that overrides the EU AI Act

questions.explanation: A, B and C are correct: the **EU AI Act and GDPR bind**; the **NIST AI RMF and OECD principles are voluntary**; **ISO/IEC 42001 is certifiable**. D is false - ISO/IEC 42001 is a voluntary international standard, not binding EU law, and it does not override the Act.

scenarios.title: Classify an AI CV-screener and map its obligations
scenarios.tagline: Run the tier ladder, then assign each duty to the right operator.
scenarios.context: A recruitment-software vendor has built an AI system that ranks and filters job applicants for its enterprise customers. Your customer, a large employer, is about to deploy it across the EU. Walk the EU AI Act analysis: which tier, which obligations, and who carries them.

steps.prompt: First decision: which EU AI Act risk tier does this applicant-screening system fall into?

choices.text: High-risk - employment/worker-management is an Annex III use case
choices.rationale: AI for recruitment and worker management is expressly an Annex III high-risk use case, so the full high-risk obligation set is in play.

choices.text: Unacceptable-risk - screening people with AI is prohibited
choices.rationale: Nothing on the Article 5 prohibited list bans AI recruitment; treating high-risk as banned is the classic error.

choices.text: Limited-risk - it just needs a notice that AI is used
choices.rationale: Transparency-only is for chatbots/deepfakes; an employment decision system carries the far heavier high-risk regime.

steps.prompt: The vendor developed the system and sells it under its own brand; your customer will use it. Who is the provider and who is the deployer?

choices.text: The vendor is the provider; the employer is the deployer
choices.rationale: The vendor develops and markets it under its own name (provider, Art 3(3)); the employer uses it under its authority (deployer, Art 3(4)).

choices.text: The employer is the provider because it will make the hiring decisions
choices.rationale: Making decisions with a system doesn't make you its provider; developing/marketing it does. The employer is the deployer - unless it re-brands or substantially modifies the system.

choices.text: Both are deployers; there is no provider in this chain
choices.rationale: Every high-risk system has a provider - here, the vendor. The provider carries the bulk of the obligations.

steps.prompt: The employer asks what IT specifically must do as the deployer (as opposed to the vendor). What's the best answer?

choices.text: Use the system per the provider's instructions, assign trained human oversight, monitor it, keep logs, and - as an employer/essential-context deployer - consider a fundamental-rights impact assessment
choices.rationale: These are the deployer duties: follow instructions for use, operationalise human oversight, monitor/report, retain logs, and (for certain Annex III deployers) run a FRIA.

choices.text: Draw up the technical documentation and perform the conformity assessment before launch
choices.rationale: Technical documentation and conformity assessment are provider obligations, not the deployer's.

choices.text: Nothing - once the vendor has done its conformity assessment, the deployer has no obligations
choices.rationale: Deployers have their own real duties (oversight, monitoring, instructions, logs); they are not obligation-free.

steps.prompt: Data-protection asks whether GDPR is engaged, given the AI Act analysis is done. Best response?

choices.text: Yes - the system profiles applicants' personal data, so GDPR applies in parallel (lawful basis, DPIA, minimisation, Article 22 automated-decision rights); the AI Act does not switch GDPR off
choices.rationale: Profiling personal data engages GDPR independently of the AI Act - a DPIA is very likely required, plus lawful basis, minimisation, and automated-decision safeguards. Non-discrimination law may also apply.

choices.text: No - the EU AI Act supersedes GDPR for AI systems, so only the Act applies
choices.rationale: The AI Act sits on top of GDPR; it does not replace it. Personal data in the system is still fully subject to GDPR.

choices.text: Only if the applicants explicitly complain
choices.rationale: GDPR obligations attach to the processing itself, not to whether anyone complains.

scenarios.title: Pick the right instrument for each governance need
scenarios.tagline: Binding vs voluntary vs certifiable - match the tool to the ask.
scenarios.context: You advise a fintech that deploys AI in the EU and builds on a large foundation model. Leadership fires questions at you across a week. For each, choose the instrument (or law) that most directly answers the need - and don't reach for a binding law when a voluntary framework is what's being asked, or vice versa.

steps.prompt: 'We operate in the EU. What must we legally CONFORM to for our high-risk AI?'

choices.text: The EU AI Act - the binding regulation whose high-risk obligations you must meet
choices.rationale: Legal conformity for AI in the EU = the EU AI Act (plus GDPR for personal data). It is the binding instrument.

choices.text: The NIST AI RMF - adopt it and you are legally compliant in the EU
choices.rationale: NIST's framework is voluntary US guidance; it confers no EU legal conformity.

choices.text: The OECD AI Principles, as the governing EU law
choices.rationale: The OECD principles are voluntary soft law, not the binding EU regime.

steps.prompt: 'We want a structured but VOLUNTARY process to manage AI risk internally - functions we can operate day to day.'

choices.text: The NIST AI RMF - Govern/Map/Measure/Manage give you a voluntary operating process
choices.rationale: The AI RMF's four core functions are exactly a voluntary, operable risk-management process.

choices.text: ISO/IEC 22989 - it defines AI terms, so it will run your risk process
choices.rationale: 22989 is a vocabulary standard; it doesn't provide a risk-management process.

choices.text: The EU AI Act, run voluntarily
choices.rationale: The Act's high-risk process is rigorous, but it is a binding legal regime, not the 'voluntary internal framework' being asked for - NIST fits the ask better.

steps.prompt: 'A regulated customer wants independent proof - a CERTIFICATION - that we govern AI responsibly.'

choices.text: Pursue ISO/IEC 42001 certification of your AI management system
choices.rationale: ISO/IEC 42001 is the certifiable AI-management-system standard - the instrument that yields auditable, independent certification.

choices.text: Publish your alignment to the OECD AI Principles as a certification
choices.rationale: The OECD principles are not a certification scheme; alignment is self-asserted soft law.

choices.text: Point to your voluntary NIST AI RMF adoption as the certification
choices.rationale: Using the NIST RMF is good practice and evidence of maturity, but it is not itself an accredited certification - 42001 is what gets certified.

steps.prompt: 'The model we build on is a very large foundation model. Who handles the model-level GPAI duties?'

choices.text: The foundation-model provider handles Article 53 (and Article 55 if it's systemic-risk); we handle our own downstream/high-risk duties
choices.rationale: GPAI model duties sit with the model provider; your fintech carries its own obligations for whatever system it builds and deploys. Two distinct roles.

choices.text: We inherit all the model provider's Article 53/55 duties automatically
choices.rationale: Downstream users don't automatically assume the model provider's GPAI duties; those stay with the provider (you get documentation to meet *your* obligations).

choices.text: Nobody - GPAI models are entirely outside the EU AI Act
choices.rationale: GPAI models are explicitly governed by Articles 51-55; they are not outside the Act.

labs.title: EU AI Act classification + obligation-mapping worksheet
labs.goal: Produce a one-page artefact that classifies a real AI system into its risk tier, assigns provider/deployer roles, maps each obligation to its owner, flags parallel laws (GDPR/non-discrimination/product-liability/IP), and stamps the timeline.
labs.steps:
## What you'll do

Take one real AI use case and produce a **one-page EU AI Act classification + obligation map** - the exact artefact a governance function would file. Use a concrete system (e.g. an AI that triages customer-support tickets, screens CVs, or scores loan applications).

1. **Describe the system in three lines** - purpose, who builds it, who uses it, what data it touches. Name the intended context of use.
2. **Run the tier ladder** (from Lesson 1) in writing:
   - Is it a **prohibited** Article 5 practice? (If yes, stop - it's banned.)
   - Is it **Annex I safety-component** or an **Annex III use case**? Check the Article 6(3) filter (narrow task? profiling?).
   - Does it **interact with people or generate synthetic content** (Article 50 transparency)?
   - Otherwise **minimal**.
   Record the tier **and the reason**, citing the route (e.g. 'Annex III - access to essential services').
3. **Assign the roles** - name the **provider** and the **deployer** (and importer/distributor if relevant). Note any role-flip risk (does the deployer modify or re-brand it?).
4. **Map the obligations to owners.** If high-risk, build a table: for each of the seven obligations (risk mgmt / data governance / technical documentation / logging / instructions-transparency / human oversight / accuracy-robustness-cyber), write **who owns it** (provider vs deployer) and **one sentence** on what satisfying it looks like here. If limited-risk, list the applicable Article 50 disclosures.
5. **Add the parallel-law line.** In one row each, note whether **GDPR** (personal data / DPIA), **non-discrimination**, **product-liability**, or **IP** law is also engaged, and why.
6. **Timeline stamp.** Note, from the implementation timeline, the date the relevant obligations start to apply (prohibitions Feb 2025 / GPAI Aug 2025 / Annex III high-risk 2 Dec 2027 / Annex I high-risk 2 Aug 2028 — the two high-risk dates were deferred from the original Aug 2026 / Aug 2027 by the Digital Omnibus, agreed May 2026; re-verify against EUR-Lex once it is published in the Official Journal).

## Why it matters

Classification-plus-obligation-mapping is *the* Domain II skill and a real deliverable. Doing it end to end - tier, reason, roles, owners, parallel law, timeline - is exactly what an exam scenario compresses into one question, and what a regulator would expect to see on file.
checklist: System described (purpose, builder, user, data, context of use)
Risk tier recorded with the reason and the classification route (Article 5 / Annex I / Annex III / Article 50 / minimal)
Provider and deployer named, with any role-flip risk noted
Each applicable obligation mapped to its owner (provider vs deployer) with a one-line 'what good looks like'
Parallel laws (GDPR, non-discrimination, product liability, IP) flagged where engaged
Relevant obligations stamped with the date they start to apply on the implementation timeline

labs.title: Critique a model card against EU AI Act obligations
labs.goal: Review an AI system's documentation/model card against the high-risk obligation set, Article 50 transparency, GDPR, and (for foundation models) GPAI duties - producing findings and remediations, the way a governance reviewer would.
labs.steps:
## What you'll do

**Critique a model/system card against EU AI Act high-risk documentation and transparency expectations** - a governance artefact reviewers actually produce. Obtain any AI system's public model card or documentation (a foundation-model card works well), or use one your team drafted.

1. **Set the standard.** From Lesson 2, list the seven high-risk provider obligations and the Article 50 transparency duties as your review checklist.
2. **Coverage pass.** For each obligation, mark the card **Present / Partial / Absent**:
   - Intended purpose and **limitations** clearly stated? (feeds instructions-for-use, Art 13)
   - **Data governance** - training-data provenance, representativeness, known **bias** and mitigations? (Art 10)
   - **Accuracy/robustness** metrics and their test conditions declared? (Art 15)
   - **Human-oversight** guidance - how a person should supervise, when to override/stop? (Art 14)
   - **Logging / traceability** described? (Art 12)
   - **Risk management** - identified risks and residual-risk treatment? (Art 9)
   - **Transparency** - does it disclose the system is AI, and (if generative) how outputs are marked as synthetic? (Art 50)
3. **GDPR pass.** Does the card address **personal data** (lawful basis, minimisation, whether a **DPIA** was done, retention, transfers)? Note gaps.
4. **GPAI pass (if a foundation model).** Does it include a **training-content summary** and a **copyright policy** statement (Article 53)? If it's a very large model, any **systemic-risk** evaluation / red-teaming (Article 55)?
5. **Write three findings.** For each gap, write a one-line **finding** and a **remediation** ('Absent: no bias evaluation - add representativeness stats and disparate-impact testing per Art 10').
6. **Verdict.** In two sentences, state whether this documentation would support a high-risk conformity story, and the single biggest gap.

## Why it matters

Documentation critique is how governance teams turn the Act's obligations into evidence - and the exam repeatedly asks whether a given artefact satisfies a given obligation. Practising the map (card section -> article -> present/absent) trains precisely that judgement.
checklist: Review checklist built from the seven high-risk obligations plus Article 50
Each obligation marked Present / Partial / Absent against the card, tied to its article
GDPR pass done (personal data, DPIA, retention, transfers) with gaps noted
GPAI pass done where relevant (training-content summary, copyright policy, systemic-risk red-teaming)
At least three findings written, each with a concrete remediation
A two-sentence verdict naming the single biggest documentation gap

resources.title: IAPP AIGP Body of Knowledge & Exam Blueprint
resources.annotation: The authoritative Domain II outline (blueprint 19-23 questions, weight ~0.25). Extract the sub-domains verbatim: II.A existing data-privacy laws (notice/consent/purpose limitation, minimisation, privacy-by-design, DPIAs, third-party processors, cross-border transfers, data-subject rights, breach notification, biometrics), II.B other existing laws (non-discrimination, consumer protection, product liability, IP), II.C the EU AI Act (risk classification 'prohibited/high/limited/minimal', the high-risk requirements, GPAI, enforcement/penalties, provider/deployer/importer/distributor), II.D industry standards (OECD principles, NIST AI RMF & Playbook, NIST ARIA, ISO/IEC 22989 & 42001). Note the current BoK is v2.1 (effective 2 Feb 2026, supersedes 2.0.1); domain titles and question ranges are unchanged from v2.0.1.

resources.title: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (EU AI Act) - Official Journal (EUR-Lex)
resources.annotation: The primary legal text. Use it to ground the four-tier structure, the Article 3 operator definitions, the high-risk obligation articles (9-15), Article 50 transparency, and the GPAI articles (51-55). The binding source of truth for every EU-AI-Act claim in this domain.

resources.title: EU AI Act - Article 3 (Definitions)
resources.annotation: Official Act text reproduced with article navigation. Extract the definitions of 'provider' (3(3), the developer placing a system on the market under its own name), 'deployer' (3(4), using a system under its authority), 'general-purpose AI model' (3(63)), 'general-purpose AI system' (3(66)), and 'systemic risk' (3(65)). The provider-vs-deployer distinction is the most-tested item in Domain II.

resources.title: EU AI Act - Article 5 (Prohibited AI practices)
resources.annotation: The unacceptable-risk tier. Extract the prohibited practices: harmful manipulation, exploitation of vulnerabilities, social scoring, predictive policing based solely on profiling, untargeted facial-image scraping, emotion recognition in workplace/education, biometric categorisation of sensitive attributes, and real-time remote biometric ID in public for law enforcement (with narrow exceptions). These are banned outright - no compliance path.

resources.title: EU AI Act - Article 6 (Classification of high-risk AI systems) + Annex III
resources.annotation: How a system becomes high-risk: Article 6(1) safety-component/product route (Annex I harmonisation legislation + third-party conformity assessment) and Article 6(2) Annex III use-case route (biometrics, critical infrastructure, education, employment, essential services, law enforcement, migration/borders, justice/democracy). Extract the Article 6(3) filter (narrow-task exception, but profiling is always high-risk).

resources.title: EU AI Act - Article 10 (Data and data governance)
resources.annotation: A high-risk obligation. Extract the standard for training/validation/testing data: 'relevant, sufficiently representative, and to the best extent possible, free of errors and complete', plus bias examination and the limited permission to process special-category data for bias detection under safeguards. Note the exam trap: the Act requires 'sufficiently representative', NOT perfect/100%-error-free data.

resources.title: EU AI Act - Article 12 (Record-keeping / logging)
resources.annotation: The high-risk logging obligation: systems must technically allow the automatic recording of events (logs) over their lifetime, to identify risk situations and substantial modifications and to support post-market monitoring. Contrast with GDPR: if those logs contain identifiers they are also personal data.

resources.title: EU AI Act - Article 14 (Human oversight)
resources.annotation: High-risk human-oversight obligation. Extract that systems must be designed for effective oversight (understand capacities/limitations, counter automation bias, interpret output, override, and stop via a 'stop button'). Key exam point: the provider designs the oversight capability while the deployer assigns the humans - a shared duty. Note the two-person verification rule for certain biometric systems.

resources.title: EU AI Act - Article 50 (Transparency obligations)
resources.annotation: The limited-risk transparency tier. Extract: inform people they are interacting with an AI system/chatbot (unless obvious); mark synthetic audio/image/video/text as artificially generated in machine-readable form; deployers disclose deepfakes and inform persons subject to emotion recognition/biometric categorisation; disclose AI-generated public-interest text. Mostly provider-for-generation, deployer-for-use.

resources.title: EU AI Act - Article 53 (Obligations for providers of GPAI models)
resources.annotation: Baseline GPAI provider duties: technical documentation; information/documentation to downstream providers; a policy to comply with EU copyright law; and a publicly available detailed summary of training content. Extract the free-and-open-source exemption from the documentation duties - and its limit (it does NOT apply to systemic-risk models, and the copyright/training-summary duties still stand).

resources.title: EU AI Act - Article 55 (Obligations for GPAI models with systemic risk)
resources.annotation: The systemic-risk extras on top of Article 53: model evaluation including adversarial testing (red-teaming); assess and mitigate systemic risks at Union level; track, document and report serious incidents to the AI Office without undue delay; ensure adequate cybersecurity for the model and its infrastructure. Pairs with Article 51's 10^25-FLOP presumption.

resources.title: EU AI Act - Article 51 (Classification of GPAI models with systemic risk)
resources.annotation: The systemic-risk trigger: a GPAI model is presumed to have high-impact capabilities (hence systemic risk) when cumulative training compute exceeds 10^25 floating point operations (FLOP); the Commission may also designate by decision, and may adjust the threshold via delegated act. Extract the exact FLOP threshold - it is a common quotable fact.

resources.title: EU AI Act Implementation Timeline (artificialintelligenceact.eu)
resources.annotation: The phased dates - the currency-critical facts: entry into force 1 Aug 2024 (nothing applies yet); prohibitions + AI literacy 2 Feb 2025; GPAI models + governance + penalties 2 Aug 2025; stand-alone Annex III high-risk 2 December 2027; Article 6(1)/Annex I high-risk 2 August 2028 (pre-2025 GPAI compliance deadline remains 2 Aug 2027). Anchor the settled dates prohibitions=Feb 2025, GPAI=Aug 2025. CURRENCY CAVEAT (as of 2026-07-02): the EU AI Act timeline is in legislative flux — the Digital Omnibus on AI (provisional agreement 6–7 May 2026; publication in the Official Journal expected shortly) DEFERS the two high-risk dates from the original 2 Aug 2026 (Annex III) / 2 Aug 2027 (Annex I) to 2 Dec 2027 / 2 Aug 2028; prohibitions and GPAI are unchanged. Re-verify the current timeline and legislative status against live EUR-Lex / European Commission sources before asserting any specific phase-in date.

resources.title: NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)
resources.annotation: The landing page for the voluntary NIST AI RMF (released 26 Jan 2023). Confirm it is 'intended for voluntary use', the four core functions (Govern/Map/Measure/Manage), the AI RMF Playbook companion, the Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1, Jul 2024), and the seven trustworthy-AI characteristics. Ground the 'voluntary, not mandatory' exam point here.

resources.title: NIST AI RMF - Core (the four functions)
resources.annotation: The AI RMF Core detail. Extract each function's job: GOVERN (cross-cutting - cultivates a risk-management culture, infused throughout), MAP (establishes context to frame risks and identify them), MEASURE (quantitative/qualitative tools to analyse, assess, benchmark and monitor AI risk), MANAGE (allocate resources to mapped/measured risks; plan responses and recovery). GOVERN is the cross-cutting one the exam asks about.

resources.title: ISO/IEC 42001 - Artificial intelligence management system (AIMS)
resources.annotation: The certifiable AI-management-system standard (2023). Confirm it specifies requirements to establish, implement, maintain and continually improve an AI management system within organisations - a management SYSTEM (process), in the same certifiable family as ISO 9001/27001, NOT a certification of any single model's accuracy. The exam contrast: 42001 certifies process; it does not certify a model. (Fetch may be blocked by ISO bot protection; corroborate via the BoK II.D naming.)

resources.title: ISO/IEC 22989 - AI concepts and terminology
resources.annotation: The AI terminology/vocabulary standard. Establishes concepts and definitions (what 'AI system', 'machine learning', 'training data' mean) - a reference standard, not a certifiable management system. The exam contrast: 22989 = terminology; 42001 = management system. (Fetch may be blocked by ISO bot protection; corroborate via the BoK II.D naming, which pairs '22989 and 42001'.)

resources.title: OECD AI Principles
resources.annotation: The voluntary intergovernmental principles (adopted 2019, updated 2024; basis for the G20 principles; first intergovernmental AI standard). Extract the five value-based principles: (1) inclusive growth/sustainable development/well-being; (2) human rights and democratic values incl. fairness and privacy; (3) transparency and explainability; (4) robustness, security and safety; (5) accountability. Soft law - influential but not directly enforceable.
